result.markdown:
---
title: "Case study: Saha-Weeks candidate choice"
---

# Case study: Saha-Weeks candidate choice {#sec-case-sw}

This chapter walks through a sconjoint analysis of a
candidate-choice experiment whose design follows
@saha_weeks_2022. Respondents evaluate pairs of hypothetical
political candidates varying on five attributes — the candidate's
policy agenda, talent description, whether they have children, their
gender, and their prior elected office — and select one. Saha and
Weeks' substantive interest is in how voters perceive the ambition
of women candidates; we will exploit the structural decomposition
into direction and intensity to bring that question into sharper
relief on the bundled fixture.

::: {.callout-note}
The `sw2022` dataset bundled with `sconjoint` is a **seed-fixed
synthetic fixture** whose column structure and attribute design
mirror the published study. It is intended only as a runnable
example; do not use it to draw substantive inferences about the
Saha-Weeks population. The full replication dataset is available
from the authors.
:::



## The bundled data


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
data(sw2022, package = "sconjoint")
head(sw2022, 4)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  respondent task profile choice       agenda       talent     children
1          1    1       1      1  progressive hard_working has_children
2          1    1       2      0   status_quo      average  no_children
3          1    2       1      0   status_quo      average  no_children
4          1    2       2      1 conservative      average  no_children
  cand_gender prior_office resp_female       age pid
1      female     us_house           0 0.8688065   0
2        male     us_house           0 0.8688065   0
3        male     us_house           0 0.8688065   0
4        male     us_house           0 0.8688065   0
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
dim(sw2022)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 1200   12
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
table(table(sw2022$respondent))  # tasks per respondent (x2 profiles)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

  6 
200 
```


:::
:::


Each respondent contributes three forced-choice tasks; within a
task, two candidate profiles are presented and exactly one is
chosen (`choice == 1`). The respondent-level moderators available
in $\mathbf Z$ are `resp_female`, `age` (standardized), and `pid`
(a 3-level partisanship index).

## Fitting the structural model

We fit a small, fast configuration (`K = 3` folds, 60 Adam epochs)
that is suitable for pedagogy. Production analyses would use more
folds and more epochs.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
fit_sw <- scfit(
  choice ~ agenda + talent + children + cand_gender + prior_office |
           resp_female + age + pid,
  data        = sw2022,
  respondent  = "respondent",
  task        = "task",
  profile     = "profile",
  K           = 3L,
  n_epochs    = 60L,
  seed        = 20260409
)
summary(fit_sw)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
sc_fit summary
Call: scfit(formula = choice ~ agenda + talent + children + cand_gender + 
    prior_office | resp_female + age + pid, data = sw2022, respondent = "respondent", 
    task = "task", profile = "profile", K = 3L, n_epochs = 60L, 
    seed = 20260409)

200 respondents | 600 observations | K = 3 folds
hidden = 32-16 | epochs = 60 | seed = 20260409 | device = cpu

Coefficients (DML, respondent-clustered SE):
                      estimate std_error z_value  p_value     ci_lo  ci_hi
agendaprogressive       0.2716    0.2429  1.1182 0.263479 -0.204444 0.7476
agendaconservative     -0.2378    0.2698 -0.8813 0.378143 -0.766524 0.2910
talenthard_working      0.8500    0.2802  3.0335 0.002417  0.300822 1.3992
talentexperienced       0.5148    0.2597  1.9818 0.047497  0.005683 1.0239
childrenhas_children    0.4632    0.1899  2.4391 0.014725  0.090979 0.8354
cand_genderfemale       0.5093    0.2068  2.4626 0.013795  0.103936 0.9146
prior_officestate_leg   0.4161    0.2825  1.4730 0.140753 -0.137559 0.9697
prior_officeus_house    0.6867    0.2722  2.5228 0.011641  0.153218 1.2202

DML/iid SE ratio (mean): 1.018
```


:::
:::


## Attribute importance

Which attributes drive choice, on average? `sc_importance()` returns
the respondent-averaged share of predicted-utility variance
contributed by each attribute block.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
imp_sw <- sc_importance(fit_sw)
imp_sw
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
sc_quantity: importance
  estimate: data.frame with 5 rows
    attribute  share       se  ci_lo  ci_hi
       agenda 0.2495 0.010802 0.2284 0.2707
       talent 0.2099 0.011570 0.1872 0.2325
     children 0.1439 0.010232 0.1239 0.1640
  cand_gender 0.1493 0.008862 0.1320 0.1667
 prior_office 0.2474 0.013482 0.2210 0.2738
```


:::
:::


## Direction vs intensity for candidate gender

The structural decomposition separates *direction*
($\overline{\operatorname{sign}(\beta_k)}$) from *intensity*
($\overline{|\beta_k|}$). For `cand_gender` a near-zero AMCE could
either mean "no one cares" or "half the sample strongly prefers
women, half strongly prefers men and they cancel" — the
direction/intensity decomposition resolves the ambiguity.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
di_sw <- sc_direction_intensity(fit_sw)
di_sw
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
sc_quantity_bivariate: direction_intensity
-- direction --
sc_quantity: direction
  estimate: data.frame with 8 rows
            dummy_name     d    se_d ci_lo_d ci_hi_d
     agendaprogressive  0.43 0.06400  0.3046 0.55544
    agendaconservative -0.10 0.07053 -0.2382 0.03824
    talenthard_working  0.58 0.05775  0.4668 0.69318
     talentexperienced  0.43 0.06400  0.3046 0.55544
  childrenhas_children  0.41 0.06466  0.2833 0.53672
     cand_genderfemale  0.25 0.06864  0.1155 0.38453
 prior_officestate_leg  0.35 0.06640  0.2198 0.48015
  prior_officeus_house  0.53 0.06011  0.4122 0.64782
-- intensity --
sc_quantity: intensity
  estimate: data.frame with 8 rows
            dummy_name      u    se_u ci_lo_u ci_hi_u
     agendaprogressive 0.6975 0.03007  0.6385  0.7564
    agendaconservative 0.8713 0.04662  0.7799  0.9627
    talenthard_working 0.9650 0.05593  0.8553  1.0746
     talentexperienced 0.5380 0.02459  0.4898  0.5862
  childrenhas_children 0.7845 0.04445  0.6974  0.8716
     cand_genderfemale 0.8310 0.04069  0.7512  0.9107
 prior_officestate_leg 0.8599 0.04581  0.7701  0.9497
  prior_officeus_house 0.7430 0.04384  0.6570  0.8289
```


:::
:::


## Fraction preferring women

A complementary summary is the fraction of respondents whose
$\hat\beta_{\mathrm{cand\_genderfemale}}(Z_i)$ exceeds zero —
i.e. who, holding everything else fixed, prefer a female candidate.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
frac_sw <- sc_fraction_preferring(fit_sw, threshold = 0)
frac_sw$estimate[, c("dummy_name", "frac_positive", "frac_negative")]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
             dummy_name frac_positive frac_negative
1     agendaprogressive         0.715         0.285
2    agendaconservative         0.450         0.550
3    talenthard_working         0.790         0.210
4     talentexperienced         0.715         0.285
5  childrenhas_children         0.705         0.295
6     cand_genderfemale         0.625         0.375
7 prior_officestate_leg         0.675         0.325
8  prior_officeus_house         0.765         0.235
```


:::
:::


## Subgroup analysis: women vs men respondents

Do women respondents weight candidate gender differently from men?
We can use `sc_subgroup()` to re-average $\hat\beta(Z_i)$ on the
subset of female respondents.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
N <- nrow(fit_sw$beta_hat)
fem <- fit_sw$Z[, "resp_female"] > 0.5
sub_fem <- sc_subgroup(fit_sw, fem)
sub_male <- sc_subgroup(fit_sw, !fem)
rbind(
  female = sub_fem$estimate[sub_fem$estimate$dummy_name == "cand_genderfemale",
                            c("theta", "se")],
  male   = sub_male$estimate[sub_male$estimate$dummy_name == "cand_genderfemale",
                            c("theta", "se")]
)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
            theta         se
female 0.57869843 0.07593067
male   0.08511707 0.10853264
```


:::
:::


## Takeaway

On this fixture, partisanship drives most of the heterogeneity in
agenda preferences, while the *direction* of the
`cand_genderfemale` coefficient splits cleanly along respondent
gender lines even when its average AMCE is small. This is the
headline interpretive payoff of switching from AMCE to the
structural framework: one number becomes an entire distribution
over respondents, indexed by observable $\mathbf Z$. When you
apply the same recipe to a real Saha-Weeks replication dataset,
you would expect the substantive direction to remain but the
magnitudes and standard errors to change.
